elements.sent-2411-001: Potentials arising in ultraviolet-completed field theories can be devoid of singularities, and hence render spacetimes simply connected.
elements.sent-2411-002: This challenges the notion of topological invariants considered in such scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-003: We explore the classical implications for (i) electrodynamics in flat spacetime, (ii) ultrarelativistic gyratonic solutions of weak-field gravity, and (iii) exact electro-vacuum black hole solutions in general relativity.
elements.sent-2411-004: In linear theories, regularity spoils the character of topological invariants and leads to radius-dependent Aharanov--Bohm phases, which are potentially observable for large winding numbers.
elements.sent-2411-005: In general relativity, the physics is richer: The electromagnetic field can be regular and maintain its usual topological invariants, and the resulting geometry is a Reissner--Nordstr\"om black hole with a spacetime region of coordinate radius $\sim q^2/(GM)$ cut out.
elements.sent-2411-006: This guarantees the regularity of linear and quadratic curvature invariants ($\mathcal{R}$ and $\mathcal{R ^2$), but does not resolve singularities in invariants such as $\mathcal{R ^p\Box^n \mathcal{R ^q$, reflected by conical or solid angle defects.}}}
elements.sent-2411-007: This motivates that gravitational models beyond general relativity need to be considered.
elements.sent-2411-008: These connections between regularity (= UV properties of field theories) and topological invariants (= IR observables) may hence present an intriguing avenue to search for traces of new physics and identify promising modified gravity theories.
elements.sent-2411-009: The field configurations for point sources in classical field theory typically contain singularities, which can render physical observables such as field strengths or self energies infinite.
elements.sent-2411-010: Physically, this points towards an inconsistency of the theory under consideration, and it is expected that in so-called ultraviolet (UV) complete theories such singularities are absent.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, finding such theories can be quite difficult and one hence often deals instead with effective field theories that regularize singularities with a regulator scale, corresponding to a UV cutoff $\Lambda < \infty$ or a minimal length $\ell > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, in the absence of a guiding principle for the form of such effective field theories it becomes all the more important to search for reliable and robust experimental signatures of such regulators.
elements.sent-2411-013: Unfortunately, quite often such effects are suppressed strongly by the typical energy of the system ($E/\Lambda \lll 1$) or by the size of it ($\ell/L \lll 1$).
elements.sent-2411-014: In this paper, we consider a different type of effect: the deformation of topological invariants by non-singular field theories.
elements.sent-2411-015: If a physical observable can be expressed through topological quantities (such as asymptotic fluxes, more on that below), its value can usually be measured rather robustly, since deformations of the measurement surface or distance do not affect its theoretical value.
elements.sent-2411-016: Conversely, if such a topological relation were to be altered, it could give rise to unique experimental signatures, such as distance-dependent charges or winding number-dependent topological phases.
elements.sent-2411-017: We will report on such violations in the context of classical field theory in a few examples and briefly point out their possible significance for experimental investigations.
elements.sent-2411-018: For instance, consider the electric field of a point charge in flat spacetime, \begin{equation}F = \frac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\frac{1}{r^2} \dd t \wedge \dd r \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-019: Its charge is given by the surface integral \begin{equation}\label{eq:intro-charge} Q = \epsilon_0 \oint_{S^2_r} \star F = \oint_{S^2_r} \left(\frac{Q}{4\pi}\frac{1}{r^2}\right) \left(r^2 \sin\theta \right) \dd\theta \wedge \dd\varphi \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-020: Key to this relation is the precise cancellation between how the field strength decreases with distance, and how the surface area of a 2-sphere $S^2_r$ grows with radius.
elements.sent-2411-021: In other words, we may think of the charge of a point particle as a topological invariant.
elements.sent-2411-022: Suppose now that we work in a theory of electrodynamics wherein the field of a point charge is non-singular, such that \begin{equation}F_reg = \frac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0} F_reg(r) \, \dd t \wedge \dd r \, ,\end{equation} where $F_\text{reg}$ is some regular function that is finite as $r \rightarrow 0$, and that asymptotically behaves as $F_\text{reg (r \rightarrow\infty) \approx 1/r^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-023: If we were to compute the charge of that configuration, we find instead a radius-dependent quantity, \begin{equation}Q(r) = \epsilon_0 \oint_{S^2_r} \star F_reg = Q - \delta Q(r) \not= Q \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-024: In such a theory, one only recovers the charge at large distances, $\delta Q(r\rightarrow\infty) = 0$, and its topological nature is lost.
elements.sent-2411-025: If we want to recover a topological notion of charge in such a non-singular theory, we need to move beyond flat spacetime and consider the charge's back-reaction onto the background metric.
elements.sent-2411-026: In other words: the surface element of a sphere around a charge cannot scale with $r^2$, because then no precise cancellation can occur.
elements.sent-2411-027: Before discussing the curved spacetime treatment, it is instructive to consider a range of examples to motivate the generality and scope of the considered problem.
elements.sent-2411-028: Hence, for the remainder of this paper, we will focus on the following three scenarios:.
elements.sent-2411-029: Electrodynamics: non-singular field theories alter the topological notion of electric charge and winding number around a solenoid in flat spacetime, and spoil the electromagnetic duality in vacuum.. [-1.4\baselineskip].
elements.sent-2411-030: Weak-field gravity: ultrarelativistic objects (``gyratons'') are described by topological invariants in the direction transverse to their motion.
elements.sent-2411-031: Again, these charges and winding numbers will lose their topological meaning in non-singular field theories.. [-1.4\baselineskip].
elements.sent-2411-032: General relativity: Electro-vacuum black hole solutions (extendable to the rotating case with cosmological constant) can feature non-singular electromagnetic fields with properly defined topological charges.
elements.sent-2411-033: This induces regular gravitational linear and quadratic curvature, at the cost of divergences of higher-order invariants.
elements.sent-2411-034: In settings where large winding numbers can be measured accurately or where field fluxes are measured as functions of distance, these scenarios hence provide avenues to test UV physics with IR observations via the ``smoking gun'' signature of deformed topological invariants.

elements.section-2411-004: Extended sources and the shell theorem in gravity
elements.sent-2411-129: Before we delve into nonlinear theory, let us address the case of extended spherically symmetric sources in Newtonian gravity, and, later, its non-singular generalization.
elements.sent-2411-130: The Poisson equation of Newtonian gravity is \begin{equation}\nabla^2 \phi = 4\pi G \rho \, ,\end{equation} which implies the shell theorem for spherically symmetric mass distributions:.
elements.sent-2411-131: The gravitational field outside a spherically symmetric mass distribution is given by the inverse-square low featuring the total mass contained inside that sphere.. [-1.4\baselineskip].
elements.sent-2411-132: The gravitational field inside a hollow sphere with spherically symmetric mass distribution vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-133: Similar theorems hold in field theories with inverse-square laws, such as electrostatics.
elements.sent-2411-134: However, if one instead considers a non-singular field theory governed by the equation \begin{equation}f(\ell^2\nabla^2) \nabla^2 \phi = 4\pi G \rho \, ,\end{equation} the above theorem loses its validity due to the departure of the $1/r^2$-behavior of the gravitational force.
elements.sent-2411-135: At the level of the Green functions, this can be illustrated by \begin{equation}\nabla^2 G_d(r) = - \, f^{-1}(\ell^2 \nabla^2) \, \delta{}^{(d)}(\ts{x}) \equiv - \, \delta{}^{(d)}_\ell(\ts{x}) \, , \tag{\ref*{eq:gf-f-2}}\end{equation} where $\delta{ ^{(d) _\ell(\ts{x )$ denotes a nascent $\delta$-function that reduces to the distributional one in the limit of $\ell\rightarrow 0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-136: For example, Eq.~\eqref{eq:f-choice implies (see also appendix \ref{app:gf ) \begin{equation}\label{eq:nascent-delta} \delta{}^{(d)}_\ell(\ts{x}) = \frac{1}{(4\pi\ell^2)^{d/2}} e^{-\ts{x}^2/(4\ell^2)} \, .\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-137: Applied to the spherically symmetric case, a slight adjustment has to be made since a radial variable only assumes positive values, and one obtains \begin{equation} \delta{}^{(1)}(r-r_0) = \frac{c(r_0,\ell)}{\sqrt{4\pi}\ell} e^{-(r-r_0)^2/(4\ell^2)} \, , c(r_0,\ell) = 2 \left[ 1 + erf\left( \frac{r_0}{2\ell} \right) \right]^{-1} \end{equation} Normalization issues aside, we note that in non-singular field theories featuring form factors $f(\ell^2\nabla^2)$ we can utilize standard Green function methods applied to an effective matter density \begin{equation}\rho_\ell(\ts{x}) = \int\dd^d x' \, \delta{}^{(d)}_\ell(\ts{x}-\ts{x}') \, \rho(\ts{x}') \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-138: This implies that even if a matter distribution has no support in a certain region (i.e.~$\rho(\ts{x ) = 0$) one may still have $\rho_\ell(\ts{x )\not=0$ in that same region.}}
elements.sent-2411-139: Putting this to the test, let us consider the mass density of an infinitesimally thin shell of mass $M$ and radius $r_0$, \begin{equation}\rho(r) = \frac{M}{4\pi r_0^2} \delta{}^{(1)}(r-r_0) \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-140: The effective density is hence \begin{equation}\rho_\ell(r) = \frac{M}{4\pi r_0^2} \frac{c(r_0,\ell)}{\sqrt{4\pi}\ell} e^{-(r-r_0)^2/(4\ell^2)} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-141: We then have for the gravitational force per unit mass \begin{equation}\frac{1}{m} F_N(r) = - \frac{G M}{r^2} \Theta_\ell(r,r_0) \, , \Theta_\ell(r,r_0) = \frac{c(r_0,\ell)}{\sqrt{4\pi}\ell r_0^2} \int\limits_0^r \dd \bar{r} \, \bar{r}^2 e^{-(\bar{r}-r_0)^2/(4\ell^2)} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-142: Explicit evaluation gives \begin{equation} \frac{\Theta_\ell(r,r_0)}{c(r_0,\ell)} = \frac{r_0^2+ 2\ell^2}{2r_0^2}\left[ erf\left( \frac{r-r_0}{2\ell} \right) + erf\left( \frac{r_0}{2\ell} \right) \right] + \frac{\ell}{\sqrt{\pi}r_0} e^{-r_0^2/(4\ell^2)} - \frac{\ell}{\sqrt{\pi}r_0} \left( 1 + \frac{r}{r_0} \right) e^{-(r-r_0)^2/(4\ell^2)} \, , \end{equation} where we factored our the normalization $c(r_0,\ell)$ to the left-hand side for notational brevity.
elements.sent-2411-143: First, we note the following property: \begin{equation}\lim\limits_{\ell\rightarrow 0} \Theta_\ell(r,r_0) =  1 ~ : ~ r > r_0 0 ~ : ~ r < r_0  \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-144: In the limit of vanishing of $\ell$ we recover standard, singular field theory, and it is a simple statement of the shell theorem: inside the hollow shell, the gravitational force vanishes.
elements.sent-2411-145: However, the case of $\ell > 0$ is more interesting.
elements.sent-2411-146: Namely, in the limit of large distances, $r \gg r_0$, we do not simply recover $\Theta_\ell = 1$, but rather \begin{equation}\lim\limits_{r\rightarrow\infty} \Theta_\ell(r,r_0) = \left( 1 + \frac{2\ell^2}{r_0^2} \right) \frac{1 + erf\left( \frac{r_0}{2\ell} \right)}{2} + \frac{\ell e^{-r_0^2/(4\ell^2)}}{\sqrt{\pi}r_0} \nonumber \approx 1 + \frac{2\ell^2}{r_0^2} + exp. suppressed in  \ell \, .\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-005: Electro-vacuum black hole solutions in general relativity
elements.sent-2411-150: Based on our previous considerations, it is clear that the area of 2-spheres must scale differently than $r^2$ at small distances to account for the improved short-distance behavior of point sources we seek to study.
elements.sent-2411-151: At the same time, this different scaling can then preserve the notion of a topologically invariant charge $Q$.
elements.sent-2411-152: Let us hence explore the notion of topological invariants in the context of black holes in general relativity coupled to electromagnetism, wherein the curvature of spacetime may allow for such a deformation of surface elements.
elements.sent-2411-153: In order to automatically guarantee the proper scaling, we imagine the non-singular field strength of a point charge scale with the radius $r$ via the function $F(r) > 0$.
elements.sent-2411-154: Then, we make the following ``$Q$-preserving'' ansatz: \begin{equation} \label{eq:bcf-ansatz} g = -B(r)\dd t^2 + C(r)\dd r^2 + \frac{1}{F(r)} \dd\Omega^2 \, , \dd\Omega^2 = \dd\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta \, \dd\varphi^2 \, , \sqrt{-g} = \frac{\sqrt{BC}\sin\theta}{F} \, , F = \frac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0} F(r) \, \dd t \wedge \dd r \, . \end{equation} This ansatz guarantees sure that the charge contained in a 2-sphere of radius $r$ is independent of $F$, \begin{equation} \label{eq:charge-0} \epsilon_0 \oint_{S^2_r} \star F = \oint_{S^2_r} \frac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0} F \, \star \left( \dd t \wedge \dd r \right) = \epsilon_0 \oint_{S^2_r} \frac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0} F \, \frac{\sqrt{BC}\sin\theta}{F} \dd\theta \wedge \dd\varphi = Q \, \sqrt{BC} \, . \end{equation} We will see shortly that $BC$ has to be set to unity in accordance with the electromagnetic field equations, which then fully implements the invariant notion of the charge $Q$ as a topological invariant even if the field $F(r)$ does not scale as $1/r^2$ for small distances.

elements.sent-2411-159: The equation for $F$ is solved immediately, and the remainder then gives a solution for $B$: \begin{equation} F(r) = \frac{1}{(r+c_1)^2} \, , B(r) = 1 + \frac{c_2}{r+c_1} + \frac{q^2}{(r+c_1)^2} \, . \end{equation} The function $B(r)$ has the following large-$r$ expansion: \begin{equation}B = 1 + \frac{c_2}{r} + \frac{q^2 - c_1 c_2}{r^2} + O\left(\frac{1}{r^3}\right) \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-160: We shall denote $c_1 = \ell$ and we read off $c_2 = -2GM$ from the asymptotic expansion for large $r$.
elements.sent-2411-161: As such, we parametrize our final solution as follows \begin{equation} \label{eq:final} \dd s^2 = -B(r) \, \dd t^2 + \frac{\dd r^2}{B(r)} + (r + \ell)^2\dd\Omega^2 \, , \ts{F} = \frac{Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0} F(r) \, F(r) = \frac{1}{(r+\ell)^2} \, , B(r) = 1 - \frac{2GM}{r+\ell} + \frac{q^2}{(r+\ell)^2} \, , \quad q^2 = \frac{GQ^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \, . \end{equation} This corresponds to the Reissner--Nordstr\"om solution of general relativity with a radial variable shifted by $\ell$.

elements.sent-2411-163: Let us now explore the properties of this metric.
elements.sent-2411-164: First and foremost, as $r > 0$ in our considerations, this metric is finite in its entire coordinate range.
elements.sent-2411-165: This can be verified, for example, by the curvature invariants \begin{equation}C{}_{\mu\nu\rho\sigma}^2 = \frac{48[GM(r+\ell)-q^2]^2}{(r+\ell)^8} \, , ~ R{}_{\mu\nu}^2 = \frac{4 q^4}{(r+\ell)^8} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-166: In order to simplify the subsequent discussions, let us express all dimensionful quantities in units of $GM$, \begin{equation}\hat{r} = \frac{r}{GM} \, , \quad \hat{\ell} = \frac{\ell}{GM} \, , \quad \hat{q} = \frac{q}{GM} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-167: This metric has two horizons located at \begin{equation}\hat{r}_\pm = 1 - \hat{\ell} \pm \sqrt{1 - \hat{q}^2} \, .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-168: For this metric to describe a black hole, we require the existence of an outer horizon.
elements.sent-2411-169: In that case, besides $\hat{q < 1$ as a necessary condition, we need to impose additionally \begin{equation}\hat{\ell} < \hat{\ell}_max = 1 + \sqrt{1 - \hat{q}^2} \, .\end{equation}.}
